pattern: Counting sequences by their final face and current run length turns the consecutive-cap rule into a small dynamic program over states.
hints: The only thing about the past that limits the next roll is which face just landed and how many times in a row it has now appeared.
Define the number of valid length-k sequences that end with face f having a run of length r; a new roll either extends that run or switches to a different face.
Track a table indexed by ending face and current consecutive count, advancing one roll at a time and taking everything modulo 1e9+7.

edge_inputs.description: a single roll has six free outcomes
two rolls where no face may repeat
every face allows a long run so no sequence is forbidden
three rolls where caps interact across faces
answer grows large enough to wrap the modulus

stress.description: moderate horizon with random per-face caps
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 300))
    rollMax = [rng.randint(1, 15) for _ in range(6)]
    return [n, rollMax]
stress.note: kept under a few hundred rolls so the brute oracle stays feasible
